title: Streams, Super Streams, and Workload Choice · Go Semantics Case: Superstream Partitioning
summary: A Go 1.26 service team must select queue, stream, or super stream from retention, replay, throughput, partition, client, and operations evidence; reviewers reject hidden goroutine ownership, unbounded work, stale channel state, and claims that stop at a nil client error. The learner predicts values, ownership transitions, and stop paths before seeing a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the superstream partitioning increment in a queue-versus-stream Go client decision record. Learners prog…
objectives: Use super streams to partition throughput by key while accounting for partitions, routing, per-partition order, skew, and consumer coordination; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.
Define age and byte retention, segment behavior, start offset, consumer progress, replay authorization, and late-reader expectations; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: RabbitMQ and Go Predict Superstream Partitioning · Theory Superstream Partitioning
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go messaging team handles case 2139 during stream-versus-queue architecture board. Build deterministic Go evidence to select queue, stream, or super stream from retention, replay, throughput, and partition evidence; keep message identity out of metric labels while preserving causal traces; then reuse a message identity with a different payload fingerprint. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. amqp091-go, AMQP sockets, RabbitMQ, TLS, partitions, restarts, Docker, databases, race and leak checks, load, upgrades, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.
